title: What is a variable
instruction:
Imagine a box with a label on it.

You write "name" on the outside. You put "Alex" inside. Whenever you need that name, you just say the label — and Go hands you what's inside.

That is a variable. A labeled box that holds a value.

Run this program and see it in action.
successMessage: You stored a value and retrieved it. That is what every variable in every program ever written does.

title: Create your own variable with :=
instruction:
`:=` is how you create a variable and give it a value at the same time.

Left side: the label. Right side: what goes inside the box.

Change the name to yours and run it.
hint: Replace "Alex" with your actual name. Keep the quotes.

codeChecks.message: Change the value from "Alex" to your own name.

successMessage: Your first original variable. := creates it and fills it in one shot.

title: Declare first, assign later
instruction:
Sometimes you want to create the box first and fill it later.

`var name string` creates an empty box labeled name.
`name = "Alex"` puts something inside.

Notice: filling later uses `=` not `:=`

Run this.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var name string
	name = "Alex"
	fmt.Println(name)
}
successMessage: Two separate steps: create the box, then fill it. This comes in handy when you do not have the value yet.

title: The var keyword with a value
instruction:
`var` can also declare and assign in one line — just like `:=` but more explicit.

`var age int = 25`

This says: create a variable called age, it holds whole numbers, and it starts at 25.

Change the age to your real age and run it.
hint: Change the number 25 to your actual age. Keep everything else the same.

codeChecks.message: Keep the var keyword and int type. Just change the number.

successMessage: var is the longer form. := is the shortcut. Both work — use whichever feels clearer.

title: Update a variable
instruction:
Variables can change. That is literally what the word means.

But there is one rule:
- `:=` creates a new variable. Use it once.
- `=` updates an existing one. Use it after.

The score starts at 60. Update it to 95 and print both values.
hint: After fmt.Println(score), write: score = 95  then fmt.Println(score) again.

codeChecks.message: Update score using a single =  like this: score = 95
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Same box, new value. = refills. := creates. Never mix them up.

title: Two variables in one line
instruction:
Go lets you declare and assign multiple variables at once.

`first, last := "Alex", "Rivera"`

Left side: the labels. Right side: the values. Order matches.

Change the names to yours and run it.
hint: Replace "Alex" with your first name and "Rivera" with your last name.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	first, last := "Alex", "Rivera"
	fmt.Println(first)
	fmt.Println(last)
}

codeChecks.message: Keep the multi-assignment format: first, last := ...
Change the names from "Alex" and "Rivera" to your own.

successMessage: One line, two variables. Go matches left to right: first gets the first value, last gets the second.

title: Declare a group with var ()
instruction:
When you have several variables, you can declare them as a group.

```
var (
    name string = "Alex"
    age  int    = 25
    city string = "London"
)
```

Clean, organized, easy to read.

Add a fourth variable called `country` with your country, then print all four.
hint: Add country string = "YourCountry" inside the var block. Then add fmt.Println(country) at the bottom.

codeChecks.message: Add country inside the var block: country string = "YourCountry"
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: The var block keeps all your declarations together. Much easier to scan than a pile of := lines.

title: Zero values
instruction:
What happens if you create a variable but never put anything in it?

Go does not leave it empty. It gives it a default value called a zero value.

- `string` gets `""`  (empty text)
- `int` gets `0`
- `bool` gets `false`

Run this and see the zero values printed.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var name string
	var score int
	var active bool
	fmt.Println(name)
	fmt.Println(score)
	fmt.Println(active)
}
successMessage: No surprises in Go. Every variable always has a value, even before you assign one.

title: Fix the broken names
instruction:
Variable names have rules:

- Start with a letter, not a number
- No spaces — use camelCase instead
- Case matters: `score` and `Score` are different variables

This code has three broken names. Fix them so the program runs.
hint: 1st name starts with a number. 2nd name has a space. 3rd is the wrong case when printed.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	1stPlace := "Gold"
	full name := "Alex Rivera"
	UserScore := 99
	fmt.Println(1stPlace)
	fmt.Println(full name)
	fmt.Println(userScore)
}

codeChecks.message: Fix the invalid variable names. No numbers at the start, no spaces — use camelCase.

successMessage: Good eye. Valid names keep your code readable and error-free.

title: Build a profile from scratch
instruction:
Everything you just learned — now use it all.

Build a short profile using these rules:
- Use `:=` for name and age
- Use `var` with a type for city
- Declare score with var and assign it separately
- Print all four

No starter code. Just you.
hint: name := "...", age := 25, var city string then city = "...", var score int then score = 100. Then four Println calls.

codeChecks.message: Declare city using the var keyword
Declare score using the var keyword

successMessage: You used every form of variable declaration in Go. Next up: the different kinds of data variables can hold.
